theory.intro: A point $(x, y)$ **lies on** a line when its coordinates make the line's equation true. To check, substitute the point's $x$-value into the equation, work out the result, and compare it with the point's $y$-value.
facts: Substitute the $x$-value of the point into the right-hand side of the equation.
If the result equals the point's $y$-value, the point **satisfies** the equation and lies on the line.
If the result does not equal the point's $y$-value, the point does not lie on the line.
Every point on the graph of a line satisfies the equation; every point off the line does not.

foundation.question_text: Does the point $(2, 7)$ lie on the line $y = 3x + 1$?
foundation.solution_text:
$3x + 1$
$= 3(2) + 1$
$= 7$
This equals the $y$-value $7$, so $(2, 7)$ lies on the line.

foundation.question_text: Does the point $(3, 5)$ lie on the line $y = x + 2$?
foundation.solution_text:
$x + 2$
$= (3) + 2$
$= 5$
This equals the $y$-value $5$, so $(3, 5)$ lies on the line.

foundation.question_text:
The line $y = 2x + 1$ is graphed, with the point $(2, 5)$ marked. Show by substitution whether $(2, 5)$ lies on the line.
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}\begin{axis}[width=6cm,height=5.4cm,axis lines=middle,axis line style={-{Stealth}},xmin=-1,xmax=4,ymin=-2,ymax=8,xtick={-1,0,1,2,3,4},ytick={-2,0,2,4,6,8},grid=both,clip=false,scaled ticks=false,ticklabel style={font=\small,/pgf/number format/fixed},xlabel={$x$},ylabel={$y$}]\addplot[thick,<->,domain=-0.5:3.3,samples=2] {2*x+1};\addplot[only marks,mark=*,mark size=2pt] coordinates {(2,5)};\node[above left] at (axis cs:2,5) {$(2,5)$};\end{axis}\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
foundation.solution_text:
$2x + 1$
$= 2(2) + 1$
$= 5$
This equals the $y$-value $5$, so $(2, 5)$ lies on the line.

foundation.question_text: Does the point $(4, 9)$ lie on the line $y = 2x$?
foundation.solution_text:
$2x$
$= 2(4)$
$= 8$
This does not equal the $y$-value $9$, so $(4, 9)$ does not lie on the line.

foundation.question_text: Does the point $(1, 6)$ lie on the line $y = 4x + 3$?
foundation.solution_text:
$4x + 3$
$= 4(1) + 3$
$= 7$
This does not equal the $y$-value $6$, so $(1, 6)$ does not lie on the line.

development.question_text: Does the point $(5, 7)$ lie on the line $y = 2x - 3$?
development.solution_text:
$2x - 3$
$= 2(5) - 3$
$= 7$
This equals the $y$-value $7$, so $(5, 7)$ lies on the line.

development.question_text: Does the point $(-2, 1)$ lie on the line $y = x + 3$?
development.solution_text:
$x + 3$
$= (-2) + 3$
$= 1$
This equals the $y$-value $1$, so $(-2, 1)$ lies on the line.

development.question_text: Does the point $(3, -4)$ lie on the line $y = -2x + 2$?
development.solution_text:
$-2x + 2$
$= -2(3) + 2$
$= -4$
This equals the $y$-value $-4$, so $(3, -4)$ lies on the line.

development.question_text: Which of the points $(3, 6)$ and $(4, 10)$ lies on the line $y = 2x$?
development.solution_text:
$(3, 6): \quad 2(3) = 6$
$(4, 10): \quad 2(4) = 8$
Since $8 \ne 10$, only $(3, 6)$ lies on the line.

development.question_text:
The line $y = x + 1$ is graphed, with the point $(2, 4)$ marked. Show by substitution whether $(2, 4)$ lies on the line.
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}\begin{axis}[width=6cm,height=5.4cm,axis lines=middle,axis line style={-{Stealth}},xmin=-1,xmax=5,ymin=-1,ymax=6,xtick={-1,0,1,2,3,4,5},ytick={-1,0,1,2,3,4,5,6},grid=both,clip=false,scaled ticks=false,ticklabel style={font=\small,/pgf/number format/fixed},xlabel={$x$},ylabel={$y$}]\addplot[thick,<->,domain=-1:4.7,samples=2] {x+1};\addplot[only marks,mark=*,mark size=2pt] coordinates {(2,4)};\node[above left] at (axis cs:2,4) {$(2,4)$};\end{axis}\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
development.solution_text:
$x + 1$
$= (2) + 1$
$= 3$
This does not equal the $y$-value $4$, so $(2, 4)$ does not lie on the line.

mastery.question_text: The point $(4, k)$ lies on the line $y = 3x - 5$. Find the value of $k$.
The point $(-2, k)$ lies on the line $y = 5x + 4$. Find the value of $k$.
Does the point $(-2, 7)$ lie on the line $y = -3x + 1$?
mastery.solution_text:
$-3x + 1$
$= -3(-2) + 1$
$= 6 + 1$
$= 7$
This equals the $y$-value $7$, so $(-2, 7)$ lies on the line.
